content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


September 12, 2001


LeRoy Swiney, Councilmember
City of Reedley
260 Dovewood Circle
Reedley, California 93654

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-206

Dear Councilmember Swiney:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

May you participate in a vote on a decision to allow the construction of a new Wal-Mart store even though the proposed site for the Wal-Mart is located within 500 feet of your residence? If you are prohibited from voting on the decision, may you appear before the city council as a private citizen?

CONCLUSION

Because the proposed site for the Wal-Mart store is located within 500 feet of your residence and no exception applies, you are disqualified from making, participating in making or influencing a decision on whether to approve the proposed location. You are, however, permitted to appear at a public meeting of the city council as a member of the general public to represent yourself on matters solely related to your personal property interests.

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, sections 18000-18997. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-01-206
Page 2

FACTS²

You are a council member for the City of Reedley. Currently, an application by the Wal-Mart Corporation regarding a proposed site on which to locate a new store in the City of Reedley is being reviewed by the city's planning commission. The planning commission may recommend for or against the approval of the application. Once the planning commission has reviewed the application and given its advice on the matter, the decision of whether to grant the application will come before the city council for a final decision. You anticipate that the matter will come before the city council for a vote on October 16, 2001, and have written for advice on whether you can participate in the decision since the proposed site for the location of the Wal-Mart is located with 500 feet of your residence.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions help to insure that public officials perform their duties impartially, free from bias attributable to their own financial interests or those of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision within the meaning of the Act if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public in general, on one or more of the official's economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted the step-by-step analysis employed below in deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Steps One and Two: Are you a "public official" subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest rules and, if you are a "public official," are you making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?

These first two steps are met under your facts. As a council member for the City of Reedley, you are a "public official." (Sections 82048, 82041). By deliberating and voting on the planning commission's recommendation you would be engaging in conduct subject to the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions. (Regulation 18702.1(a)(1).)

___________________________

² These questions and facts reflect additional information you provided in our telephone conversations of September 1, 2001, September 5, 2001, and September 6, 2001.

File No. A-01-206
Page 3

Step Three: What are your pertinent economic interests?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from
economic interests. The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are
defined in section 87103 and regulations 18703-18703.5. Under these provisions, a public
official is deemed to have an economic interest in his or her personal residence if the official's
interest in the residence is valued at $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b), Regulation 18703.2.)
Assuming that your interest in your residence is worth at least $2,000, you have an economic
interest in your home for purposes of section 87103. You have not identified any other economic
interests that might give rise to a conflict of interest.

Step Four: Are your economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?

Where an official has a real property interest that is located within 500 feet of property
that is the subject of a governmental decision, the real property is considered to be directly
involved in the decision. (Regulation 18704.2(a).) Your home is within 500 feet of the property
that is the subject of the upcoming Wal-Mart location decision. Therefore, under regulation
18704.2(a), your real property interest is directly involved in this decision.

Steps Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on your personal
residence be deemed to be material and reasonably foreseeable?

Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official must
evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on any of those economic interests. Regulation 18705.2 sets forth the materiality standards
that apply to economic interests in real property. The materiality thresholds vary depending
upon the distance between the official's real property and the real property that is the subject of
the decision. As noted above, your home is located within 500 feet of the proposed site for the
Wal-Mart. Under subdivision (a) of regulation 18705.2, the effect of a decision on real
property is presumed to be material if the real property is within 500 feet of the real property that
is the subject of the decision, in this case the proposed site of the Wal-Mart. This presumption
may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
have any financial effect on the real property. (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1).) Absent facts
establishing proof that the presumption is rebutted or applicability of one of the exceptions to
disqualification set forth below, you may not participate in the decision of whether or not to
allow Wal-Mart to establish a new store in the proposed location.

File No. A-01-206
Page 4

Step Seven: Does the "Public Generally" exception apply?

Under what is called the "public generally" exception, an official may still participate in a
decision if the financial effect of the decision on the official's economic interest is
indistinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation
18707(a).) This "public generally" exception is codified in regulations 18707-18707.9. Pursuant
to these provisions, if a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction is affected by the governmental
decision in substantially the same manner as the decision would affect the public official, then
the official may participate in the decision.

The Commission's regulations contain specific "public generally" provisions which apply
to decisions in small jurisdictions with a population of 25,000 or less. (Regulation 18707.3.)
According to your facts, the City of Reedley meets this condition of having a population of fewer
than 25,000; however, application of this "small jurisdiction" regulation also requires that:

"(3) The real property that serves as the public official's principal residence is
more than 500 feet from the boundaries of the property which is the subject of the
decision." (Regulation 18707.3(a)(3).)

Since your residence is located within 500 feet of the property that is the subject of the
decision concerning the Wal-Mart, the "public generally" exception for small jurisdictions will
not apply.

In the absence of a more specific "public generally" regulation applying to your situation,
we turn to the general rule provided by regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(B) which provides that, in
regard to real property, a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction is comprised of:

"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents; or

(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's
agency."

In our telephone conversation, you estimated that there are only 30 – 40 homes similarly
located to yours in relation to the proposed store site, so subdivision (ii) cannot apply. To meet
subdivision (i), those 30 – 40 homes would have to constitute 10 percent or
more of all property owners or all homeowners in the City of Reedley. Since this does not
appear to be the case, subdivision (i) will likewise not apply. Therefore, our conclusion does not
change; you are presumed to have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the site of the
new store.

File No. A-01-206
Page 5

Step Eight: Legally Required Participation

The eighth step of the conflict-of-interest analysis pertains to the "legally required participation" rule. (See Regulation 18708.) This rule generally applies when several public officials in the same agency are simultaneously disqualified. Since we do not have facts to suggest that your participation will be legally required, we conclude our analysis here.

Appearing Before the City Council

In response to your question about appearing before the city council after being disqualified from voting on the decision regarding whether or not to permit the construction of a Wal-Mart store on the proposed site, you are prohibited from attempting to influence members of the city council about the decision. Under regulation 18702.3(a) (copy enclosed), you would be attempting to use your official position with the city council to influence the decision, if you contacted or appeared before, "or otherwise attempted to influence" any member, officer, employee, or consultant of the city council or any agency under the budgetary control of the city council. However, you are permitted to appear at a public meeting of the city council as a member of the general public to represent yourself on matters solely related to your personal property interests as outlined in regulation 18702.4(a)(2) (copy enclosed). "Personal interests" include an interest in real property solely owned by the official or his or her immediate family. (Regulation 18702.4(b)(1)(A).) The exception does not apply if you represent other homeowners in this process.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Adrianne Korchmaros
Political Reform Consultant
